title: Rate hold expectations on slowdown
full_text:
Inflation slowed to 4.2 per cent in April as the Albanese government's halving of the fuel excise limited the rise in petrol prices, boosting economists' expectations that the Reserve Bank of Australia will hold interest rates in June.
New data released by the Australian Bureau of Statistics on Wednesday showed the headline inflation rate declined slightly from 4.6 per cent in March, lower than forecasts of 4.4 per cent, even as new figures showed the Middle East war is pushing up some prices beyond the bowser.
Fuel prices fell 7 per cent in April after the excise was slashed to 26.3¢. The relief measure, expected to cost the government more than $2.5 billion in revenue, started on April 1 as bowser prices rocketed due to the Middle East conflict.
Treasurer Jim Chalmers said inflation would have risen to 4.7 per cent without the cut to the fuel excise. He said the decision would take the "sting" out of inflation, but cautioned that inflation would continue to spread.
"[We] know there's still an inflation challenge in our economy. There was before the war, but the war in the Middle East has turbocharged that inflation challenge, and that's why the budget was so responsible," he told a press conference in Canberra.
Economists argue this month's federal budget did not do enough to stymie the growth of government spending in the near-term to help take the heat out of Australia's economy.
Labor is spending more than it is saving by an extra $5.3 billion in the current financial year, and an additional $6.5 billion in 2026-27.
The main inflation drivers in April were the cost of housing (up 6.3 per cent) and transport (up 6.6 per cent). Housing inflation reflected rising costs for electricity after the expiry of federal and state government rebates, which cost the government $6.8 billion in foregone revenue between 2022 and 2025.
Public transport prices also fell 20 per cent over the month due to the Victorian government's provision of free public transport in April, which cost an estimated $71 million in foregone revenue. The state will continue to provide free buses, trains, and trams in May.
Government interventions were making inflation data more difficult to interpret than usual, KPMG chief economist Brendan Rynne said.
Rynne said while the excise cut and free public transport were artificially lowering headline inflation, the unwinding of federal and state government electricity rebates was pushing up annual price growth by around 0.4 percentage points.
"This is making it difficult for the RBA to get a read on the numbers at this point," he said. "[It] could hint at a notable rise in petrol prices in the months ahead, fuelling inflation even further now the government's help has tapered off."
Economists say despite early signs, inflation is starting to bleed into the economy beyond the bowser. They continue to expect the RBA will keep interest rates on hold at 4.35 per cent at its June 16 meeting after three consecutive increases this year.
Money markets were predicting a 13 per cent chance of a rate rise in June before the new data was released.
Commonwealth Bank of Australia senior economist Trent Saunders said while the impact of inflation on the wider economy was "limited" in April, it would continue over the coming months. "[There] remains a risk that the pass-through has been delayed rather than avoided. Higher construction and other business input costs could still feed into CPI inflation over coming months," he said.
Challenger chief economist and ex-RBA official Jonathan Kearns agreed the second-round effects would add to price pressures in the economy over the coming months, but Wednesday's data gave the RBA space to leave rates on hold.
Underlying inflation - which excludes the most volatile price movements - rose 3.4 per cent, in line with economists' expectations.
Higher oil prices are starting to hit the economy beyond the bowser, said the ABS, which noted significant annual price increases for postal services (up 12.4 per cent) and new dwelling construction (up 4.7 per cent).
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Lea Jurkovic | Economics correspond
heading: Banking and Economy
